text: The surgeon removes the omentum after the small bowel is easily extracted from the hernia site.
The surgeon identifies ethobond suture material within the surgical field, likely used in previous repair attempts.
The surgeon demonstrates the hybrid component of the prior hernia repair, showing a small incision made over the hernia defect, which has been closed.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field after releasing adhesions, examining the area around the hernia site.
The surgeon identifies the superior defect located above the visible peritoneal fold.
The surgeon decides to leave the peritoneum intact at the beginning of the procedure, aiming to preserve it as a posterior layer during the hernia repair.
The surgeon begins retrorectus dissection at the inferior edge of the progrip mesh, which was placed during the initial operation.
The surgeon attempts to remove the mesh from the muscle on one side, but observes that the mesh is firmly adhered to the muscle tissue.
The surgeon attempts to separate the posterior sheath from the mesh, utilizing the presence of a well-preserved posterior sheath.
The surgeon encounters difficulty with the mesh being stuck and decides to proceed with dissection in the inferior direction where the tissue is less adherent.
The surgeon reaches the level of the umbilicus, identifying a small umbilical defect in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon continues dissection along the medial edge of the right rectus muscle, carefully separating tissue layers.
The surgeon identifies contributory perforator vessels originating from the epigastric arteries, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to keep the epigastric vessels elevated during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection to separate the posterior rectus sheath from the underlying rectus muscle.
The surgeon returns to the upper section after completing most of the inferior dissection, focusing on separating the posterior rectus sheath from the pro-grip mesh.
The surgeon marks a line towards the superior lateral edge of the hernia defect, indicating the boundary for further dissection.
The surgeon attempts to dissect laterally to free the posterior rectus sheath from the mesh, while maintaining awareness of the epigastric vessels and perforator vessels.
The surgeon continues to take down the posterior sheath using a straightforward maneuver, separating it from the underlying structures.
The surgeon observes the mesh positioned above the surgical field, indicating its placement relative to the dissected area.
The surgeon identifies a neurovascular bundle in the lateral region near the superior edge of the defect during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects the posterior sheath away from the ring of the hernia, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the hernia repair.
The surgeon continues the dissection in the upper abdomen, revealing a section of cut transversus muscle.
The surgeon inadvertently initiates an upper transverse abdominis release (TAR) in the upper abdomen, visible as a dissection of the transversus muscle.
The surgeon attempts to take down the peritoneum to improve visibility in the surgical field, addressing the confusion in the anatomical layout.
The surgeon attempts to join tissue planes in the upper abdomen, considering leaving additional peritoneum as a posterior layer.
The surgeon examines the area above to locate the edge of the defect, focusing on the anatomical structures involved in the hernia repair.
The surgeon struggles to visualize the lateral edge of the defect due to the obstructing peritoneum.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum to improve visibility of the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the posterior sheath further laterally, revealing the expanded area where the tissue has blown open.

The surgeon accesses the unviolated plane in the right lower quadrant below the arcuate line, carefully dissecting the superficial transversalis fascia to enter the TAR plane. The peritoneum is stripped down, revealing the transversus abdominis, which appears as a sheet of fascia.
The surgeon creates two or three small holes on one side of the surgical field, visible in the current view.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, sweeping the peritoneum down to maintain a bloodless field, while continuing the transversus abdominis release (TAR) towards the upper abdomen.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, moving towards the medial aspect, while maintaining a bloodless field and stripping the peritoneum down.
The surgeon transitions the dissection to take down the transverse allofascia and pre-transverse allofascia, exposing the bare muscle of the transversus abdominis.
The surgeon continues dissection, moving upwards towards a challenging area, while maintaining a bloodless field and sweeping the peritoneum down.
The surgeon identifies the nerve bundles and ensures to stay medial to them during the dissection.

The surgeon realizes that during the maneuver intended to take down the posterior sheath, the transversus abdominis muscle was inadvertently hooked, resulting in an upper TAR (Transversus Abdominis Release).
The surgeon completes the dissection of the transverse allofascia, exposing the bare muscle of the transversus abdominis.
The surgeon navigates back into the pre-transversalis plane, carefully maneuvering to maintain the correct anatomical layer during the hernia procedure.
The surgeon continues dissection in the pre-transversalis plane, working in previously undisturbed tissue space, which facilitates easier separation of anatomical layers.
The surgeon carefully retracts the peritoneum and attenuated hernia sac to expose the edge of the hernia defect, preparing to continue the transversus abdominis release.
The surgeon navigates back into the proper retro-rectus space on the opposite side, revealing an open hybrid incision over the supraumbilical defect. The defect in the upper abdomen remains unclosed.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, progressing smoothly to achieve posterior coverage of the hernia defect.
The surgeon identifies the costal margin, which is visible as a distinct anatomical landmark during the procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection smoothly, maintaining a clear separation of tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgical view moves towards the xiphoid process, which becomes visible shortly.
The surgeon reaches a high point in the dissection, where diaphragmatic fibers become visible.
The xiphoid process is visible superior to the surgeon's right arm instrument during the dissection.
The surgeon observes the completed dissection area, with clear visibility of the anatomical structures previously exposed, such as the xiphoid and diaphragmatic fibers.
The surgeon displays the cut edge of the transversus abdominis (TA) muscle, highlighting the superior and inferior defects of the hernia.
The surgeon prepares to start the left-sided dissection by placing right-sided ports, mirroring the initial setup for the procedure.
